title: Data from: Nonstationarity in wildlife disease dynamics: insights from the prairie dog–plague system
display_name: Data from: Nonstationarity in wildlife disease dynamics: insights from the prairie dog–plague system
abstract: Ecological relationships often vary in strength and direction across ecosystems, a pattern that has become increasingly evident as ecology expands to broader spatial and temporal scales. Although such nonstationarity—the changing effect of predictor variables across spatiotemporal contexts—is widely acknowledged in ecological research, it has received limited attention in the study of wildlife diseases. Here, we evaluated nonstationarity in sylvatic plague epizootics among black-tailed prairie dog (Cynomys ludovicianus) colonies across nine federally managed grasslands in the Great Plains, USA. Using a 29-year dataset (~1992–2020) and a mixed-effects machine learning approach, we assessed how the effects of climate and colony characteristics on plague varied in strength and direction at each grassland. Across sites, colony size, clustering, and structural connectedness showed generally stationary, positive associations with outbreak probability. By contrast, weather effects were strongly nonstationary: temperature and precipitation alternately increased, decreased, or showed little association with outbreaks depending on the grassland. Predictor importance also shifted across space and within epizootics; at some sites precipitation signaled outbreak onset, whereas host spatial structure governed subsequent spread and persistence. These results indicate that host aggregation provides a consistent scaffold for transmission, while environmental drivers act locally and vary through time. Recognizing this contrast can improve forecasting and mitigation, as interventions such as vaccination or vector control are likely to perform differently across environmental contexts. Methodologically, explicitly modeling slope heterogeneity identified where and when covariate effects differ, offering a transferable approach for disentangling local from broad-scale drivers of wildlife disease across heterogeneous landscapes.

title: SIREN (Finding Novel Precursors for Solar Wind Stream Interaction Regions with Interpretable Deep Learning)
display_name: SIREN (Finding Novel Precursors for Solar Wind Stream Interaction Regions with Interpretable Deep Learning)
abstract: SIREN: SIR Encoder Network — Source Code, Data, and Model Checkpoint This deposit accompanies the manuscript: "Finding Novel Precursors for Solar Wind Stream Interaction Regions with Interpretable Deep Learning" Prateek Mayank, Yogesh, Enrico Camporeale, D. Chakrabarty, Lan K. Jian, Gregory G. Howes, and Thomas E. BergerSolar wind stream interaction regions (SIRs) form at the interface between fast and slow solar wind streams and are a primary driver of recurrent geomagnetic storms. Most existing SIR catalogs rely on expert visual inspection, which is inherently subjective and yields binary labels with no measure of confidence. This work presents SIREN (SIR Encoder Network), a lightweight Transformer-based model for automated, per-timestep SIR detection from in-situ solar wind observations, with calibrated probability outputs and systematic feature attribution via Integrated Gradients. Model summary Architecture: Two-layer Transformer encoder with learned positional embeddings and a per-timestep classification head (~100,000 trainable parameters) Input: Sequences of 864 timesteps (~6 days at 10-minute cadence) across 11 solar wind parameters: |B|, Bx, By, Bz, V, Vy, Vz, Np, temperature, east-west flow angle, north-south flow angle Training data: 676 SIR events from the Chi et al. (2018) catalog (Wind spacecraft, 1995–2016); 70/15/15 train/validation/test split with fixed seed 42 Calibration: Platt scaling applied post-training on the validation set Test-set performance: ROC-AUC = 0.93, F1 = 0.78, TSS = 0.67, HSS = 0.70, Accuracy = 88.7% Reproducing the results pip install -r requirements.txt python train.py The script loads all CSV files from data_si3d_csv/, trains with a fixed random seed (42), applies Platt scaling, and saves the model checkpoint, test metrics (JSON), and training history plots.
source_display_name: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

title: Data from: Force-induced ankle opening reveals mechanical stabilization of the ankle of human β-cardiac myosin
display_name: Data from: Force-induced ankle opening reveals mechanical stabilization of the ankle of human β-cardiac myosin
abstract: Human β-cardiac myosin drives contraction in the heart. Extensive biophysical and single-molecule studies have quantified myosin’s chemo-mechanical cycle, which generates ~5 nm of displacement and 5–7 pN of force. Myosin’s 9-nm-long, α-helical lever arm is rigidified by bound essential and regulatory light chains (ELC and RLC). Numerous pathogenic mutations and sequence-conservation patterns within the lever arm where the RLC binds (LARLC) belie the overly simplified view that the lever arm acts solely as a rigid rod that transduces ATP hydrolysis into motion. Structural studies have shown that myosin adopts an interacting-heads motif (IHM), which inhibits motor activity and mechanically strains the RLC complex, consisting of the RLC bound to the LARLC. Alteration in the configuration of the RLC complex’s “ankle”—a sharp kink in the lever arm—is hypothesized to modulate the propensity of myosin to enter the IHM. To investigate the complex’s mechanical stability, we developed a single-molecule atomic-force microscopy assay with three different pulling geometries: pulling across the LARLC, the RLC, and the RLC complex. When pulling across the LARLC by applying force to its N and C termini, the mechanical dissociation of the RLC was resolved along with two intermediates. Coarse-grained Brownian dynamics detailed these molecular configurations as the opening of myosin’s ankle and the preferential dissociation of one of the RLC’s two EF-hand domains. Moreover, the linker between the EF-hand domains forms an interface with an RLC N-terminal loop. This interface stabilized the native acute ankle angle against opening. Pulling across the RLC and the RLC complex revealed different unfolding pathways, each with one intermediate. Looking forward, these assays can probe for the effects of pathogenic mutations and phosphorylation on the nanomechanics of the RLC complex.

title: Age determination of sediment cores KNR197-10-44GGC, KNR158-4-09GGC and HU87003-7PC
display_name: Age determination of sediment cores KNR197-10-44GGC, KNR158-4-09GGC and HU87003-7PC
abstract: This dataset comprises age determination for KNR197-10-44GGC, KNR158-4-09GGC and HU87003-7PC. The Tuned age model for this study was based on a tephra tie point and the correlation between 44GGC coarse fraction (%)/ice rafted detritus counts and the North Greenland Ice Core Project (NGRIP) Ca2+ with additional age constraints from 14C accelerator mass spectrometry (AMS) dates on planktonic foraminifera from early Holocene. The Tuned age model was then transferred to KNR158-4-09GGC by correlating % Neogloboquadrina pachyderma while keeping the tephra tie point, and to HU87003-7PC by correlating Neogloboquadrina pachyderma d18O. Also included in the dataset is the reservoir ages estimated by combining the 14C dates with the Tuned ages from each of the three cores.
source_display_name: Publishing Network for Geoscientific and Environmental Data (PANGAEA) (Alfred Wegener Institute for Polar and Marine Research)
host_organization_name: Alfred-Wegener-Institut Helmholtz-Zentrum für Polar- und Meeresforschung

title: Benthic foraminiferal oxygen isotopes and trace metal data of sediment core HU87003-7PC
display_name: Benthic foraminiferal oxygen isotopes and trace metal data of sediment core HU87003-7PC
abstract: This dataset comprises paleoceanographic reconstructions from HU87003-7PC using multiple proxies, including foraminiferal d18O (planktonic foraminifera: Neogloboquadrina pachyderma) analysed on a VG Prism Instrument at NOSAMS, Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution; foraminiferal trace metals (Mg/Ca, Mn/Ca, Al/Ca, Fe/Ca, B/Ca and Sr/Ca) (benthic foraminifera: Cibicides lobatulus); foraminiferal Mg/Ca derived temperature (benthic foraminifera: Cibicides lobatulus). d18O is reported relative to the VPDB standard, with analytical precision estimated to be better than ±0.07 ‰. The samples for trace metal analysis were cleaned using clay removal, reductive, oxidative, and weak acid leaching steps following established protocols. Samples with > 5 µg post-cleaning weights were analysed for a suite of trace metal elemental ratios at the ICP-MS Trace Metal Lab at Rutgers University.
source_display_name: Publishing Network for Geoscientific and Environmental Data (PANGAEA) (Alfred Wegener Institute for Polar and Marine Research)

title: Planktic foraminifera, planktic and benthic foraminiferal oxygen isotopes and trace metals, ice rafted detritus and sortable silt data of sediment core KNR197-10-44GGC
display_name: Planktic foraminifera, planktic and benthic foraminiferal oxygen isotopes and trace metals, ice rafted detritus and sortable silt data of sediment core KNR197-10-44GGC
abstract: This dataset comprises paleoceanographic reconstructions from KNR197-10-44GGC using multiple proxies, including coarse fraction percentage; ice rafted detritus counts; planktonic foraminifera abundance (shown as % Neogloboquadrina pachyderma); sortable silt grain size data (analysed on both a Beckman Coulter Multisizer 4 using the Enhanced Performance Multisizer 4 beaker and stirrer setting 30 for full sediment suspension and a Malvern Mastersizer 2000 at University College London) and inferred flow speed; foraminiferal d18O (planktonic foraminifera: Neogloboquadrina pachyderma and Globigerina bulloides; benthic foraminifera: Globobulimina affinis and Nonionellina labradorica) measured on a VG Isogas SIRA mass spectrometer with the MultiCarb preparation system (for sample size > 80 µg) and a Thermo MAT253 isotope ratio mass spectrometer with Kiel IV carbonate preparation device (for sample size < 80 µg) at the Godwin Laboratory, University of Cambridge; foraminiferal trace metals (Mg/Ca, Mn/Ca, Al/Ca and Fe/Ca) (planktonic foraminifera: Neogloboquadrina pachyderma; benthic foraminifera: Globobulimina affinis and Nonionellina labradorica); foraminiferal Mg/Ca derived temperature (planktonic foraminifera: Neogloboquadrina pachyderma; benthic foraminifera: Globobulimina affinis). d18O is reported relative to the VPDB standard, with analytical precision estimated to be better than ±0.07 ‰. The samples for trace metal analysis were cleaned using clay removal, reductive, oxidative, and weak acid leaching steps following established protocols. Samples with > 5 µg post-cleaning weights were analysed for a suite of trace metal elemental ratios at the ICP-MS Trace Metal Lab at the University of Colorado, Boulder.

Dybing, Diego Melgar, Amanda M. Thomas, Dara E. Goldberg, David Mencin, and Brendan W. Crowell submitted to Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America. title: Thwaites Eastern Ice Shelf 100 MHz Ice-Penetrating Radar Data, 2019-2020
display_name: Thwaites Eastern Ice Shelf 100 MHz Ice-Penetrating Radar Data, 2019-2020
abstract: This dataset contains 100 MHz ground-based ice-penetrating radar data collected over the Thwaites Eastern Ice Shelf (TEIS) from December 2019 through January 2020, stored as NetCDF files. Data was collected by the Thwaites-Amundsen Regional Survey and Network Integrating Atmosphere-Ice-Ocean Processes (TARSAN) team of the International Thwaites Glacier Collaboration (ITGC). Files contain both raw and processed data. Processed data includes surface elevation, theoretical hydrostatic disequilibrium, and both ice-ocean interface and relatively bright englacial layer picks. All elevation values are recorded in meters. All geographic information is referenced to the polar stereographic projection (EPSG:3031). The dataset also includes PDF files showing each profile and its location on the shelf, as well as a CSV file with information from the external GPS units that accompanied the radar units in the field.

title: Dataset Collection Informing the North American Biodiversity and Climate Change Assessment
display_name: Dataset Collection Informing the North American Biodiversity and Climate Change Assessment
abstract: Understanding the interplay between biodiversity and climate change across national boundaries is critical for the implementation of effective and lasting solutions to climate change and for maintaining biodiversity and nature’s contributions to people. The North American Biodiversity and Climate Change Assessment (BCCA) will characterize the state of understanding concerning key linkages between biodiversity and climate change, identify critical knowledge gaps, and summarize implications for biodiversity and climate change policy in the United States, Canada, Mexico, and adjacent regions as appropriate. A collection of datasets were created to evaluate the current knowledge on these topics up until 2024 and inform the development of assessment chapters. The collection of datasets evaluate available knowledge regarding climate projections, the direct effects of climate change on biodiversity, drivers of biodiversity change, their effects on ecosystem services and nature's contribution to people, and effects occurring across jurisdictional boundaries in North America. The dataset collection includes systematic literature review outputs, statistical and geospatial analysis outputs using pre-existing climate projections, species, and ecosystems datasets. These datasets were used to inform discussions on chapters 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7 of the North American Biodiversity and Climate Change Assessment.

title: Analytical Data for Multi-Stage Assembly of the Utuado Pluton, Puerto Rico: Evidence from Enclaves and Amphibole Geochemistry
display_name: Analytical Data for Multi-Stage Assembly of the Utuado Pluton, Puerto Rico: Evidence from Enclaves and Amphibole Geochemistry
abstract: This dataset contains all analytical results used in the study “Multi-stage Assembly of the Utuado Pluton, Puerto Rico: Evidence from Enclaves and Amphibole Geochemistry” that has been submitted for publication. The data integrate whole-rock major and trace element compositions, electron microprobe analyses of amphibole major elements, and LA-ICP-MS trace element analyses of amphiboles from enclaves and granodioritic host rocks within the Utuado Pluton. Whole-rock geochemical data include major elements (XRF) and trace elements (ICP-MS) for samples collected from two field localities (Cañon Blanco Utuado and Río Jayuya Utuado). These data were used to characterize magma compositions, evaluate enclave–host relationships, and assess evidence for hybridization and melt extraction within the pluton. Amphibole major element data (EPMA) document patchy zoning and intra-crystal chemical domains (Zones 1–3) that preserve discrete stages of crystallization and magma recharge. Amphibole trace element data (LA-ICP-MS) provide insight into melt evolution, mineral–melt partitioning behavior, and subduction-related geochemical signatures. Together, these datasets support thermobarometric calculations, equilibrium-melt estimates, and the interpretation of magmatic processes including recharge, mingling, hybridization, and limited melt extraction during incremental pluton assembly. All files are provided in spreadsheet format with sample identifiers, analytical conditions where applicable, and variable descriptions to facilitate reuse.

abstract: Single Sersic profile fits for 28,274 sources over Abell 2744, in all 20 NIRCam bands authors: Yunchong Zhang, Tim Miller, Sedona Price, Wren Suess, Rachel Bezanson, David Setton, and the UNCOVER/MegaScience Collaboration The sersic parameters and associated uncertainties are estimated with pysersic. The fitting targets are selected by requiring SNR>10 in the given band, using the UNCOVER/MegaScience photometric catalog. We additionally fit these observed sizes (flagged as robust or great quality) as a function of wavelength. Using the redshift posterior distribution from the UNCOVER/MegaScience SPS catalog, we infer rest-frame sizes for each available case at UV (0.3 micron), optical (0.5 micron), and NIR (1 micron), which are included in the rest-frame size catalog. See README or the catalog release paper (to be released) for details.

title: Data from: Longitudinal changes in gut microbiota across reproductive states in wild baboons
display_name: Data from: Longitudinal changes in gut microbiota across reproductive states in wild baboons
abstract: Background. In humans and other mammals, female reproduction is linked to extensive changes in physiology, immunity, hormones, and behavior. These changes likely shape, and may be shaped by, the composition of gut microbial communities. Characterizing the dynamics of gut microbial change across reproductive states, including its relationship to female physiology, is important for understanding how the gut microbiome influences female and offspring health. Results. Here we characterize longitudinal changes in gut microbiota across reproduction by combining 16S rRNA gene sequencing data from 4,462 stool samples (spanning 14 years of sample collection) with life history data on multiple reproductive events in 169 female baboons. These baboons were members of a well-studied, natural baboon population in Kenya where reproductive state (ovarian cycling, pregnancy, and postpartum amenorrhea) is tracked daily and microbiota data could be paired with measurements of fecal-derived estrogen, progesterone, and glucocorticoid levels. We found extensive changes in baboon gut microbiota as females transitioned between reproductive states. Pregnancy was linked to distinct patterns of ASV richness, community composition, and taxonomic abundances compared to postpartum amenorrhea and ovarian cycling. The most dramatic shifts occurred as females transitioned from the first to second trimester of pregnancy, with altered abundances of taxa that have been linked, in humans and model systems, to host immunity, weight gain, or hormone levels. Host identity was consistently the strongest predictor of gut microbiota composition across states, and this individual signature was strongest during pregnancy. Estrogen and progesterone levels had robust associations with the gut microbiota overall, but the microbial taxa involved in these associations are reproductive state-dependent. Glucocorticoid concentrations were not a major predictor of gut microbiota composition in any state. Conclusions. Together, our results support the idea that gut microbiota contribute to the complex physiological changes necessary during pregnancy, but that microbial changes during pregnancy are somewhat unique to each female. Variation in steroid hormones drives some, but not all, of these relationships, emphasizing the importance of considering steroid hormone levels in studies of gut microbiome variation. Our results motivate future work on how gut microbiota contribute to reproductive outcomes, including both maternal and offspring health.

title: Nutrient Network Subset for: Climate influences how belowground traits regulate grassland biomass
display_name: Nutrient Network Subset for: Climate influences how belowground traits regulate grassland biomass
abstract: This dataset contains plot-level observations of aboveground biomass, community-weighted mean (CWM) plant traits, trait coverage metrics, and environmental variables across grassland sites compiled from the Nutrient Network (NutNet). Data collection followed the standardized Nutrient Network protocols described in Borer et al. (2014). This dataset contains only data from the unmanipulated control plots (1m-2) within the Nutrient Network. For each site, we selected the year of pre-treatment data, that is, the year before the experimental treatment was started in adjacent experimental Treatment plots. All community-level belowground trait data was calculated based on community-level vegetation composition (quantified in % species coverage per plot) derived from the respective databases. All trait data derived from the UNDERPLOT database. Each row represents one plot in one year. The subset containing data from the Nutrient Network (NutNet) used in this study is published separately from the main dataset (see related works below) due to network data sharing policies. Project Personnel of the Nutrient Network (NutNet): Elizabeth Borer and Eric Seabloom; NutNet raw data used: "full-cover_2025-01-31.csv", "comb-by-plot_2025-01-31.csv" This work was generated using data from the Nutrient Network experiment, funded at the site-scale by individual researchers. Coordination and data management have been supported by funding to E. Borer and E. Seabloom from the National Science Foundation Research Coordination Network (NSF-DEB-1042132) and Long Term Ecological Research (NSF-DEB-1831944 and NSF-DEB-2425352) programs, and the Institute on the Environment (DG-0001-13). We also thank the University of Minnesota College of Biological Sciences and the Minnesota Supercomputer Institute for hosting project data and the Institute on the Environment for hosting Network meetings.

title: Data for "A Mosaic Layered Halide-Perovskite Spin Glass: Mechanochemical Alloying of a Ferromagnet and a Paramagnet"
display_name: Data for "A Mosaic Layered Halide-Perovskite Spin Glass: Mechanochemical Alloying of a Ferromagnet and a Paramagnet"
abstract: Data for "A Mosaic Layered Halide-Perovskite Spin Glass: Mechanochemical Alloying of a Ferromagnet and a Paramagnet" Pulverizing together a Cr(II) perovskite, (BA)2CrCl4, and a Cr(III) double perovskite, (BA)4AgCrCl8, at room temperature affords a new layered perovskite alloy incorporating three different metal ions in each layer: (BA)8(AgCr)Cr2Cl16 (BA = n-butylammonium). The magnetic ground state of this alloy is a spin glass (freezing temperature ~3 K), which we propose arises from intrinsic disorder of superexchange interactions and a propensity to form ferromagnetic clusters. To explore the composition space beyond this example, we model the geometrical and topological properties of these complex alloys by representing the [MCl6] tiling as effectively hard-rhombus packings on a square lattice. The structures provided by our computationally efficient model provide insight into magnetic exchange, ordering across various length scales, and the role of the in-plane Jahn–Teller distortion of the Cr(II) centers in dictating the packing within the inorganic layer. By quantifying local compositional fluctuations, we identify alloy compositions at which the mixing characteristics are appreciably different from those observed in random square-lattice mixtures of three different metals. These results demonstrate a general, mechanochemical route to two-dimensional spin glasses and provide design principles and computational tools for expanding the phase space of complex layered halide perovskites with non-trivial magnetic ground states.

title: Biocollections Data Curation Survey
display_name: Biocollections Data Curation Survey
abstract:
The study used a survey of collection managers to address the study’s purpose of understanding perspectives on curation of biocollections. The current perspectives on curation and behaviors in practice on the most important access and reuse facets could inform the design of the cyberinfrastructure tools and services to improve reusability of all physical collections and associated resulting data. The study population included collection managers and curators and other museum professionals that directly manage biocollections data. The 21 question survey included job analyses questions as well as assessing data curation tasks and perspectives on metadata. The study addressed the following research questions:
What are the characteristics of curator and collection managers?What are the storage, access, and reuse practices for biocollections?What are the challenging DarwinCore metadata implementations?Who are the users of this data?The results can inform the building of a machine-actionable research data cyberinfrastructure to increase data access and reuse for this community of practice. The study received Institutional Review Board approval (UTK IRB-25-09083-XM: Biocollections Data Curation Survey). Three big organizations/Professional Societies capture most of this population, Biodiversity Information Standards, formerly Taxonomic Data Working Group (TDWG) which is the abbreviation they still prefer, iDigBio (Integrated Digitized Biocollections) and the Society for the Preservation of Natural History Collections (SPNHC). After IRB approval in September 2025, the survey was distributed to email Listservs commonly used by collection managers and curators from those organizations and several others that are discipline specific: NHCOLL, iDigBio, Mammal, ECN (Entomology). In October 2025, a reminder was sent to the same email lists.

title: Simulation 2: DeepSoil Models, in A Quantitative and Qualitative Evaluation of Seismic Retrofits According to the Los Angeles Non-Ductile Reinforced Concrete Buildings Ordinance
display_name: Simulation 2: DeepSoil Models, in A Quantitative and Qualitative Evaluation of Seismic Retrofits According to the Los Angeles Non-Ductile Reinforced Concrete Buildings Ordinance
abstract: In this portion of the study, DeepSoil models were used to perform one-dimensional equivalent-linear site response analyses to evaluate the influence of local soil conditions on earthquake ground motions. Input ground motions were applied at the bedrock level of each soil profile and propagated through the overlying soil layers to estimate the resulting ground motion at the ground surface. This process accounts for the amplification, attenuation, and frequency-dependent modification of seismic waves as they travel through different soil deposits. Once the surface ground motions were obtained, the resulting acceleration time histories were used as input motions for structural analyses by applying them at the foundation level of the building model, which was assumed to have a fixed-base condition. To investigate the effects of subsurface conditions, two soil profiles representative of Site Class D were developed and analyzed. Each profile was subsequently evaluated using soil column depths of both 100 ft and 400 ft, resulting in a total of four distinct soil profiles for comparison. The generated surface motions provide a localized representation of seismic demand that reflects the characteristics of each soil profile rather than relying solely on generalized site-class assumptions. By comparing the resulting structural responses and performance outcomes across the four profiles, the site response analyses demonstrate how variations in soil stratigraphy and profile depth can influence the seismic behavior, expected damage, repair costs, and functional recovery of existing structures. These results provide insight into the importance of considering site-specific geotechnical conditions when assessing seismic risk and building resilience.

title: Simulation 6: Retrofit Alternatives: SP3 Models, in A Quantitative and Qualitative Evaluation of Seismic Retrofits According to the Los Angeles Non-Ductile Reinforced Concrete Buildings Ordinance
display_name: Simulation 6: Retrofit Alternatives: SP3 Models, in A Quantitative and Qualitative Evaluation of Seismic Retrofits According to the Los Angeles Non-Ductile Reinforced Concrete Buildings Ordinance
abstract: In this portion of the study, the SP3 response prediction engine was used to evaluate and compare the expected post-earthquake performance of several seismic retrofit alternatives for an existing non-ductile reinforced concrete building. The retrofit strategies considered in the analyses included reinforced concrete shear walls, buckling restrained braced frames (BRBFs), viscous dampers, fiber reinforced polymer (FRP) strengthening systems, and base-isolation systems. Unlike the detailed nonlinear dynamic analyses performed using OpenSees and STKO in other portions of the study, these assessments utilized simplified building models and SP3's response prediction capabilities to estimate structural performance and earthquake consequences directly. The SP3 response prediction engine uses building characteristics, retrofit properties, and seismic hazard information to estimate engineering demand parameters and resulting damage without requiring computationally intensive nonlinear response history analyses. These predicted responses were subsequently used to estimate damage states, indirect repair costs, and functional recovery times associated with each retrofit alternative. By using a consistent analytical framework, the retrofit strategies could be evaluated and compared under equivalent hazard conditions. The primary objective of these analyses was to assess the relative effectiveness of each retrofit alternative in reducing earthquake-related disruptions and improving post-earthquake resilience. The resulting output files include estimates of indirect repair costs, recovery times, and other resilience-related performance metrics for each retrofit strategy across multiple hazard levels. These results were subsequently used to rank the retrofit alternatives based on their expected performance, providing insight into the tradeoffs between different retrofit approaches. Through this methodology, the SP3 models offer a streamlined means of comparing retrofit technologies and identifying strategies that provide the greatest reductions in economic losses and recovery duration while enhancing overall building resilience.
source_display_name: Open MIND

title: Simulation 5: Site Response: SP3 Models, in A Quantitative and Qualitative Evaluation of Seismic Retrofits According to the Los Angeles Non-Ductile Reinforced Concrete Buildings Ordinance
display_name: Simulation 5: Site Response: SP3 Models, in A Quantitative and Qualitative Evaluation of Seismic Retrofits According to the Los Angeles Non-Ductile Reinforced Concrete Buildings Ordinance
abstract: In this portion of the study, SP3 models were used to evaluate the post-earthquake performance of the building based on structural responses obtained from the local site response analyses. Floor accelerations and interstory drifts generated from nonlinear response history analyses in OpenSees served as the primary inputs to the SP3 framework. These structural responses were produced using surface ground motions generated through DeepSoil site response analyses, allowing the resulting performance assessments to reflect the influence of site-specific soil conditions on seismic demand. The SP3 analyses provide a performance-based assessment of building resilience by quantifying expected repair costs, functional recovery times, and damage states across multiple seismic hazard levels and soil profiles. The framework combines engineering demand parameters derived from the structural analyses with component fragility relationships and consequence functions to estimate physical damage and associated recovery consequences. By incorporating both structural and nonstructural damage, the SP3 models provide a comprehensive evaluation of building performance that extends beyond traditional life-safety measures to include economic and functional impacts. To utilize these data, users must process the acceleration and drift outputs from the OpenSees analyses conducted using the DeepSoil-generated surface motions and provide them as inputs to the SP3 models. The resulting output files include estimates of repair costs, recovery times, damage states, and other resilience-related performance metrics for each soil profile and hazard level considered in the study. These results demonstrate how localized site effects can influence expected earthquake losses and recovery outcomes when compared with assessments based solely on generalized site-class assumptions. Through this approach, the SP3 models translate site-specific structural responses into decision-relevant performance measures that can be used to evaluate seismic risk, resilience, and the importance of incorporating local geotechnical conditions into performance-based earthquake engineering assessments.
source_display_name: Open MIND

title: Simulation 1: Site Class: STKO Models, in A Quantitative and Qualitative Evaluation of Seismic Retrofits According to the Los Angeles Non-Ductile Reinforced Concrete Buildings Ordinance
display_name: Simulation 1: Site Class: STKO Models, in A Quantitative and Qualitative Evaluation of Seismic Retrofits According to the Los Angeles Non-Ductile Reinforced Concrete Buildings Ordinance
abstract: In this portion of the study, conditional mean spectrum (CMS) analysis was used to select representative ground motion records corresponding to the target seismic hazard levels. These ground motions were applied at the fixed base of a three-dimensional nonlinear dynamic structural model developed in OpenSees, with STKO serving as the graphical user interface for model development, analysis execution, and output management. Nonlinear response history analyses were conducted to evaluate the seismic behavior of the structure under each selected ground motion record. The numerical simulations generated structural response outputs in the form of floor accelerations and interstory drifts at each floor and story level throughout the building. To utilize these data, the OpenSees models must first be executed and the resulting output files processed to extract the required response parameters. These acceleration and drift responses serve as the primary inputs for the Site Class SP3 performance assessments. Within the SP3 framework, the structural response data are used to estimate building damage, repair costs, and functional recovery times associated with each hazard level. The resulting SP3 outputs provide a comprehensive assessment of expected post-earthquake performance and resilience. These data can subsequently be used to evaluate how repair costs and recovery times vary across seismic hazard levels and building designs and provide a basis for comparison with future analyses that incorporate alternative ground motion characterization approaches.
source_display_name: Open MIND

title: Simulation 4: Site Class: SP3 Models, in A Quantitative and Qualitative Evaluation of Seismic Retrofits According to the Los Angeles Non-Ductile Reinforced Concrete Buildings Ordinance
display_name: Simulation 4: Site Class: SP3 Models, in A Quantitative and Qualitative Evaluation of Seismic Retrofits According to the Los Angeles Non-Ductile Reinforced Concrete Buildings Ordinance
abstract: In this portion of the study, SP3 models were used to evaluate the post-earthquake performance of the building based on the structural responses obtained from the site-class analyses. Floor accelerations and interstory drifts generated from the nonlinear response history analyses in OpenSees served as the primary inputs to the SP3 framework. These engineering demand parameters were used to estimate damage to structural and nonstructural building components, as well as the resulting consequences associated with earthquake-induced losses. The SP3 analyses provide a performance-based assessment of building resilience by quantifying expected repair costs, functional recovery times, and damage states across a range of seismic hazard levels. The framework combines structural response data with component fragility information and consequence functions to estimate the extent of damage and the resources required for repair and recovery. By evaluating both direct physical damage and recovery-related impacts, the SP3 models provide insight into how a building is expected to perform following an earthquake beyond traditional measures of life safety. To utilize these data, users must process the acceleration and drift outputs from the OpenSees analyses and provide them as inputs to the SP3 models. The resulting output files include estimates of repair costs, recovery times, and other performance metrics that can be used to evaluate building resilience under the generalized Site Class D ground motion assumptions. These results provide a baseline assessment of expected post-earthquake performance and establish a reference point for comparison with future analyses that incorporate localized site response effects. Through this approach, the SP3 models translate structural response quantities into decision-relevant performance measures that can be used to assess seismic risk, resilience, and recovery potential.
source_display_name: Open MIND

title: Simulation 3: Site Response: STKO Models, in A Quantitative and Qualitative Evaluation of Seismic Retrofits According to the Los Angeles Non-Ductile Reinforced Concrete Buildings Ordinance
display_name: Simulation 3: Site Response: STKO Models, in A Quantitative and Qualitative Evaluation of Seismic Retrofits According to the Los Angeles Non-Ductile Reinforced Concrete Buildings Ordinance
abstract: In this portion of the study, local site response analyses were incorporated to evaluate the influence of site-specific soil conditions on structural performance. Ground motions were first propagated through representative soil profiles using one-dimensional equivalent-linear site response analyses in DeepSoil. The resulting surface ground motions were then applied at the fixed base of a three-dimensional nonlinear dynamic structural model developed in OpenSees, with STKO serving as the graphical user interface for model development, analysis execution, and output management. Nonlinear response history analyses were performed to evaluate the seismic behavior of the structure under the site-specific surface motions generated for each soil profile. The numerical simulations produced structural response outputs in the form of floor accelerations and interstory drifts at each floor and story level throughout the building. To utilize these data, the OpenSees models must be executed using the surface motions obtained from the DeepSoil analyses (Simulation 2), and the resulting output files must be processed to extract the required response parameters. These acceleration and drift responses serve as the primary inputs for the SP3 performance assessments. Within the SP3 framework, the structural response data are used to estimate building damage, repair costs, and functional recovery times associated with each hazard level and soil profile. The resulting SP3 outputs provide a comprehensive assessment of expected post-earthquake performance and resilience while accounting for localized site effects. These data can subsequently be used to evaluate how repair costs and recovery times vary due to differences in subsurface conditions and site response characteristics, providing insight into the importance of incorporating site-specific geotechnical effects into seismic performance assessments.
source_display_name: Open MIND

title: High-Resolution Daily PM2.5 Dataset for the Contiguous US (2005–2021)
display_name: High-Resolution Daily PM2.5 Dataset for the Contiguous US (2005–2021)
abstract: Overview This dataset provides high-resolution (1 km) daily surface-level PM2.5 concentration estimates for the contiguous United States from 2005 to 2021. The data is organized into annual archives containing the monthly files. Methodology The estimates were generated using a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory network with an Attention mechanism. To improve estimation precision, particularly on days with extreme pollution events, the deep learning model integrates multiple data sources. These sources include in situ measurements, remotely sensed data, and wildfire smoke density observations. By leveraging the temporal dynamics of air pollution, this model demonstrates a significant improvement on high-concentration days compared to existing products. Associated Publication & Citation If you use this dataset in your research, please cite the corresponding methodology paper: Wang, Z., Crooks, J. L., Regan, E. A., & Karimzadeh, M. (2025). High-Resolution Estimation of Daily PM2.5 Levels in the Contiguous US Using Bi-LSTM with Attention. Remote Sensing, 17(1), 126. https://doi.org/10.3390/rs17010126

abstract: The dataset includes diaspore morphotype descriptions and measurements from the late Campanian (74.6 million years ago) Dori's Tuff (DT) flora in the Jose Creek Formation, New Mexico, USA. A total of 77 diaspore types have been identified from over 450 specimens. Additionally, the dataset includes the diaspore volume dataset for 59 assemblages from the Cretaceous and Cenozoic, based on Wing & Boucher's (1998) [1] data. All fossil specimens related to this dataset are reposited at the University of California Museum of Paleontology (Berkeley, CA, USA) or the Perot Museum of Nature and Science (Dallas, TX, USA).

title: Biological soil crust microcolonies reveal how microbial communities assemble following retreat of a High Arctic glacier, Ny-Ålesund, Svalbard, 2022
display_name: Biological soil crust microcolonies reveal how microbial communities assemble following retreat of a High Arctic glacier, Ny-Ålesund, Svalbard, 2022
abstract: The data are from focused sampling of discrete Biological Soil Crust (BSC) colonies near the snout of a retreating glacier (Midtre Lovénbreen) in the High Arctic and show that BSC colonies had significantly higher 16S and 18S ribosomal ribonucleic acid (rRNA) gene diversity than the simpler communities of bare sediments sampled next to each colony. The colonies also had a higher degree of community dispersion than the more clustered bare sediment controls. There were only eight 16S amplicons that showed 100% prevalence in all 12 of the colonies, and the three most abundant of these keystone amplicons were cyanobacteria, including a nitrogen fixing Nostoc. The prominence of phototrophs indicates that early-successional BSC colonies are being supported by photosynthesis rather than ancient- or aeolian-derived organic matter. Co-occurrence network analysis among the phototrophs and fungi identified several potential early-successional soil lichens. Overall, our fine-scaled sampling revealed new insights into community assembly and function in actual communities of interacting microbes (as opposed to mixed communities in bulk soil samples) during the early stages of primary succession.

title: Data from: Exhumation history of the Sangre de Cristo Range, Colorado, from mid- to low-temperature thermochronology: New insights into Laramide contraction and Oligocene–Miocene extension in the northern Rio Grande rift, USA
display_name: Data from: Exhumation history of the Sangre de Cristo Range, Colorado, from mid- to low-temperature thermochronology: New insights into Laramide contraction and Oligocene–Miocene extension in the northern Rio Grande rift, USA
abstract: This dataset consists of thermochronologic data that form the basis of a 2026 Tectonics manuscript titled “Exhumation history of the Sangre de Cristo Range, Colorado, from mid- to low-temperature thermochronology: New insights into Laramide contraction and Oligocene–Miocene extension in the northern Rio Grande rift, USA.” Data from samples collected in the Sangre de Cristo Range in southern Colorado include zircon (U-Th)/He dates (21 samples, 97 grains), apatite (U-Th)/He dates (16 samples, 73 grains), zircon fission dates (6 samples), apatite fission track dates (2 samples), K-feldspar 40Ar/39Ar data (4 samples), and biotite 40Ar/39Ar data (1 sample). These thermochronologic data provide insight into the polyphase deformation history and spatiotemporal patterns of extensional exhumation in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains in southern Colorado. Key findings based on these data include: a) The Alvarado fault along the northeastern range flank initiated as a Laramide reverse fault and was reactivated as a normal fault in the early Miocene; b) The onset of rapid cooling due to extensional exhumation is 20–16 Ma along northeastern flank of the range, 18–14 Ma near the range crest, and 14–10 Ma along southwestern flank of the range; c) The Sangre de Cristo Range underwent limited cooling between the Oligocene inception of extension and Miocene onset of rapid cooling associated with slip on range-bounding faults; and d) The southwestern flank of the range was exhumed from temperatures of ~240±25°C at the onset of Miocene rapid cooling, consistent with ~7–9 km displacement across the Sangre de Cristo fault system.
source_display_name: DRYAD

title: Biocollections Data Curation Survey
display_name: Biocollections Data Curation Survey
abstract:
The study used a survey of collection managers to address the study’s purpose of understanding perspectives on curation of biocollections. The current perspectives on curation and behaviors in practice on the most important access and reuse facets could inform the design of the cyberinfrastructure tools and services to improve reusability of all physical collections and associated resulting data. The study population included collection managers and curators and other museum professionals that directly manage biocollections data. The 21 question survey included job analyses questions as well as assessing data curation tasks and perspectives on metadata. The survey instrument is provided with the data. The study addressed the following research questions:

What are the characteristics of curator and collection managers?What are the storage, access, and reuse practices for biocollections?What are the challenging DarwinCore metadata implementations?Who are the users of this data?The results can inform the building of a machine-actionable research data cyberinfrastructure to increase data access and reuse for this community of practice. The study received Institutional Review Board approval (UTK IRB-25-09083-XM: Biocollections Data Curation Survey). Three big organizations/Professional Societies capture most of this population, Biodiversity Information Standards, formerly Taxonomic Data Working Group (TDWG) which is the abbreviation they still prefer, iDigBio (Integrated Digitized Biocollections) and the Society for the Preservation of Natural History Collections (SPNHC). After IRB approval in September 2025, the survey was distributed to email Listservs commonly used by collection managers and curators from those organizations and several others that are discipline specific: NHCOLL, iDigBio, Mammal, ECN (Entomology). In October 2025, a reminder was sent to the same email lists.

title: A Comparison of Multispectral and Hyperspectral Re-mote-sensing data from coincident flights of Crewed and Un-crewed Aircraft
display_name: A Comparison of Multispectral and Hyperspectral Re-mote-sensing data from coincident flights of Crewed and Un-crewed Aircraft
abstract: This dataset archive contains the full set of imagery, calibration data, and derived products used to evaluate the performance of two low-cost UAS multispectral sensors (MicaSense RedEdge and SlantRange 3p) relative to the NEON Imaging Spectrometer (NIS). The data include radiometrically calibrated reflectance measurements, field-based tarp calibration observations, and spectrally resampled NIS imagery used as a reference standard, along with corresponding NDVI products for each sensor platform. These datasets support analysis of systematic reflectance and vegetation index biases across multiple land cover types, providing a basis for cross-platform comparison and validation. The archive enables reproducibility of results and facilitates further investigation into sensor-specific limitations, calibration approaches, and the integration of UAS multispectral data with airborne hyperspectral observations for ecological and agricultural remote sensing applications.

title: East Greenland Ice-core Project (EGRIP) water-isotope data from 6 – 2657 meters at different resolutions measured using continuous flow analysis (CFA) reported on the Greenland Ice-Core Chronology 2005 and 2021 (GICC05 and GICC21)
display_name: East Greenland Ice-core Project (EGRIP) water-isotope data from 6 – 2657 meters at different resolutions measured using continuous flow analysis (CFA) reported on the Greenland Ice-Core Chronology 2005 and 2021 (GICC05 and GICC21)
abstract: This data set is part of a joint international effort for the East Greenland Ice-core Project (EGRIP), which has retrieved an ice core by drilling through the Northeast Greenland Ice Stream (NEGIS, 75.63°N, 35.98°W). Ice streams are responsible for draining a significant fraction of the ice from the Greenland Ice Sheet (GIS), and the project was developed to gain new and fundamental information on ice stream dynamics, thereby improving the understanding of how ice streams will contribute to future sea-level change. The drilled core also provides a new record of past climatic conditions from the northeastern part of the GIS. The project has many international partners and is managed by the Centre for Ice and Climate, Denmark with air support carried out by US ski-equipped Hercules aircraft managed through the US (United States) Office of Polar Programs, National Science Foundation. Approximately 2657 meters of ice core have been recovered from the combined efforts of drilling operations in 2017, 2018, 2019, a break for COVID in 2020 and 2021, resuming in 2022 and 2023. Analyses followed in 2024 and 2025. Here we present records of stable isotopes of oxygen and hydrogen from 21.5 meters to 2657 m depth at a variety of depth intervals/resolutions. The data products presented here have been supported by the National Science Foundation, project: Collaborative Research: The fingerprint of abrupt temperature events throughout Greenland during the last glacial period. Award # 1804098. Please see the README file for important information

title: Data and predictors for "Global Link Between Atmospheric Heat Emission and Precipitation Isotope δ18O"
display_name: Data and predictors for "Global Link Between Atmospheric Heat Emission and Precipitation Isotope δ18O"
abstract: USNIP Precipitation δ18O and ERA5-Derived Predictors (1989–2014) ================================================================== DATASET OVERVIEW ----------------- This dataset accompanies the manuscript: Ajay, A., Dar, S.S., Vachon, R., Bailey, A., & Welker, J.M. (2026). Global Link Between Atmospheric Heat Emission and Precipitation Isotope δ18O. It contains monthly precipitation δ18O observations from 73 stations of the United States Network for Isotopes in Precipitation (USNIP), covering the contiguous United States (1989-2014), matched to 59 candidate predictor variables derived from ERA5 reanalysis (Hersbach et al., 2020) and ancillary climate indices. Full predictor definitions, units, and physical descriptions are provided in Table S1 of the manuscript's Supporting Information. FILE DESCRIPTION ----------------- All_data_USNIP.csv -- 12,673 rows (monthly station-month observations) x 64 columns. ACCESS RESTRICTION ------------------- These data have historically been distributed by the USNIP program upon direct request rather than through a public repository. Access is provided here via Zenodo's request mechanism, consistent with that existing data-sharing practice. Requests are reviewed by the corresponding author. COLUMN DEFINITIONS ------------------- Site : USNIP station identifier Date : Observation month (YYYY-MM-DD, day set to 1st of month) Latitude : Station latitude (degrees N) Longitude : Station longitude (degrees E) Elevationm : Station elevation (m above sea level) d1816_PWA : Precipitation-weighted-average δ18O (per mil) -- TARGET VARIABLE TP : Total precipitation (m, ERA5 accumulated) EVAP : Evaporation (m, ERA5 accumulated) -- The following 59 columns are the candidate predictors used in the Random Forest / SHAP analysis. Full physical descriptions, units, and ERA5 vs. derived source are listed in Table S1 of the paper's Supporting Information; column names here match Table S1's "Predictor variable" entries directly. -- mean_ss_down_lw_flux_clear : DLR-cs, clear-sky downward longwave radiation mean_ss_down_lw_flux : DLR all-sky, all-sky downward longwave radiation mean_ss_down_sw_flux_clear : SWR clear-sky mean_ss_down_sw_flux : SWR all-sky surf_net_solar_rad : Net solar radiation OLR : Outgoing longwave radiation potential_evap : Potential evaporation tc_water_vapour : TCWV, total column water vapour IVT : Integrated vapour transport magnitude v_int_eastward_water_vapour_flux : IVT eastward component v_int_northward_water_vapour_flux : IVT northward component ULI_mr : (see Table S1 / manuscript Methods for definition) v_int_energy : Vertically integrated atmospheric energy ResTime : Moisture residence time (TCWV / precip rate) temp_2m : T2m, 2 m air temperature skin_temperature : Surface (skin) temperature dewpoint_temp_2m : 2 m dewpoint temperature surface_pressure : Surface pressure u10, v10 : 10 m wind components (zonal, meridional) Monthly_Precip_mm : Total monthly precipitation VPD_surf : Surface vapour pressure deficit precip_type : Precipitation phase (categorical) evaporation : Actual surface evaporation SVP_surface : Surface saturation vapour pressure e_actual_surface : Surface actual vapour pressure temp_500, temp_850 : Air temperature at 500/850 hPa spec_humidity_500, spec_humidity_850 : Specific humidity at 500/850 hPa rel_humidity_500, rel_humidity_850 : Relative humidity at 500/850 hPa geopotential_500, geopotential_850 : Geopotential at 500/850 hPa vert_velocity_500, vert_velocity_850 : Vertical velocity (omega) at 500/850 hPa Q_monthly : Net column heating P_E : Precipitation minus evaporation (TP - EVAP) SVP_850, SVP_500 : Saturation vapour pressure at 850/500 hPa bl_height_v2 : Planetary boundary-layer height surf_sensible_heat_flux : Surface sensible heat flux surf_latent_heat_flux : Surface latent heat flux Thick : 850-500 hPa geopotential thickness tcc, hcc, lcc : Total/high/low cloud cover fraction conv_precip : Convective precipitation LogDist : Log-transformed distance to nearest coast SOI, ONI, NAO, PDO, AO, PNA : Climate indices (Southern Oscillation, Oceanic Nino, North Atlantic Oscillation, Pacific Decadal Oscillation, Arctic Oscillation, Pacific-North American) G : sqrt(|DLR-cs x OLR|), derived radiative coupling term (geometric mean of DLR-cs and OLR) NOTES ----- - Missing values are encoded as empty cells in the CSV. - See the manuscript's Methods (Section 2) and Table S1 for full predictor descriptions, units, and ERA5 vs. derived provenance. CITATION -------- If you use this dataset, please cite the associated manuscript (see above) and acknowledge the USNIP program as the source of the precipitation isotope observations. CONTACT ------- Jeffrey Welker, University of Alaska Anchorage (jmwelker@alaska.edu)

Missing values are encoded as empty cells in the CSV. - See the manuscript's Methods (Section 2) and Table S1 for full predictor descriptions, units, and ERA5 vs. derived provenance. title: Supplementary Data Release for Development of Liquefaction-Targeted Design Basis for U.S. Seismic Provisions
display_name: Supplementary Data Release for Development of Liquefaction-Targeted Design Basis for U.S. Seismic Provisions
abstract: These data are supplementary to the journal article: Makdisi, A.J., Kramer, S.L., Bassal, P., Maurer, B.W., Perkins, W., Anderson, D., Crouse, C.B., Dashti, S., and Teague, D. (2026) "Development of Liquefaction-Targeted Design Basis in U.S. Seismic Provisions". Earthquake Spectra. https://doi.org/10.1002/esp4.70097. This dataset includes tabular data summarizing effective 50-year non-exceedance probabilities of liquefaction factor of safety (FSL) using current ASCE 7-22 design parameters across a range of soil, site, and liquefaction modeling conditions, for 160 test locations throughout the conterminous United States. Proposed liquefaction-targeted Maximum Considered Earthquake (MCEL) peak ground acceleration (PGAL) values, corresponding to a 2.5% in 50-year probability of liquefaction triggering, are also provided for the 160 test locations for Site Classes C, CD, D, DE, and E. Current ASCE 7-22 MCEG peak ground acceleration (PGAM) values are provided for these same locations and site classes for the purposes of comparison. Metadata for each test location (e.g., region, coordinates, population, seismic hazard levels) are also provided.

title: East Greenland Ice-core Project (EGRIP) water-isotope data from 6 – 2657 meters at different resolutions measured using continuous flow analysis (CFA) reported on the Greenland Ice-Core Chronology 2005 and 2021 (GICC05 and GICC21)
display_name: East Greenland Ice-core Project (EGRIP) water-isotope data from 6 – 2657 meters at different resolutions measured using continuous flow analysis (CFA) reported on the Greenland Ice-Core Chronology 2005 and 2021 (GICC05 and GICC21)
abstract: This data set is part of a joint international effort for the East Greenland Ice-core Project (EGRIP), which has retrieved an ice core by drilling through the Northeast Greenland Ice Stream (NEGIS, 75.63°N, 35.98°W). Ice streams are responsible for draining a significant fraction of the ice from the Greenland Ice Sheet (GIS), and the project was developed to gain new and fundamental information on ice stream dynamics, thereby improving the understanding of how ice streams will contribute to future sea-level change. The drilled core also provides a new record of past climatic conditions from the northeastern part of the GIS. The project has many international partners and is managed by the Centre for Ice and Climate, Denmark with air support carried out by US ski-equipped Hercules aircraft managed through the US (United States) Office of Polar Programs, National Science Foundation. Approximately 2657 meters of ice core have been recovered from the combined efforts of drilling operations in 2017, 2018, 2019, a break for COVID in 2020 and 2021, resuming in 2022 and 2023. Analyses followed in 2024 and 2025. Here we present records of stable isotopes of oxygen and hydrogen from 21.5 meters to 2657 m depth at a variety of depth intervals/resolutions. The data products presented here have been supported by the National Science Foundation, project: Collaborative Research: The fingerprint of abrupt temperature events throughout Greenland during the last glacial period. Award # 1804098. Please see the README file for important information

title: Biocollections Data Curation Survey
display_name: Biocollections Data Curation Survey
abstract: The study used a survey of collections professionals to address the goal of understanding data curation practices and perspectives of DwC to inform the development of a machine-actionable research data cyberinfrastructure to increase access and reuse. The study population included curators, collection managers, and other museum professionals that directly manage biocollections information. The study addressed the following major questions: What are the professional demographics of biocollections personnel? What are the data storage, management, and access practices that inform reuse for biocollections? What are the challenges associated with implementing the Darwin Core standard? The study received Institutional Review Board approval (UTK IRB-25-09083-XM: Biocollections Data Curation Survey). The target population was primarily accessed through two professional organizations: Integrated Digitized Biocollections (iDigBio) and the Society for the Preservation of Natural History Collections (SPNHC), which collectively represent a substantial proportion of biocollections professionals. Following IRB approval on September 29, 2025, the survey was distributed to natural history and discipline-specific listservs commonly used by collections personnel: iDigBio, SPNHC, Earth Science Information Partners (ESIP), GBIF North America Slack channels, the American Society of Mammalogists, and the Entomology Collections Network. A reminder was sent on November 3, 2025, to the same venues, and the survey closed on November 14, 2025. After eight weeks, a total of 57 responses were collected.

title: Cold-stage system for Impact-initiated Contact freezing Experiments (C-ICE)
display_name: Cold-stage system for Impact-initiated Contact freezing Experiments (C-ICE)
abstract: This dataset supports the manuscript “Cold-stage system for Impact-initiated Contact-freezing Experiments (C-ICE)”. The data provide the experimental and modeled results used to evaluate the C-ICE system, quantify impact-initiated contact freezing of silver iodide (AgI) aerosols, and constrain particle–droplet collision efficiency under controlled laboratory conditions.
